comments.text: Following jspier's finite-atom approach, and using the standard reduction recorded in Tao/natso's notes, I have a small formalized update: the tail part of the previous $1.8$-type lower-bound argument can be pushed to $M=1.806304$ with a five-atom certificate. The method is the usual dual-forcing one. For each moving parameter $a$, construct a positive finite measure $\lambda_a$ with $U_{\lambda_a}>0$ on the normalized support $\{-1\}\cup[0,1]$. Then $$ \int U_\mu\,d\lambda_a=\int U_{\lambda_a}\,d\mu $$ forces at least one point of $\operatorname{supp}\lambda_a$ to lie in $\{U_\mu>0\}$, and sweeping $a$ gives extra length. For the tail block I use $$ a\in[-1.806304,-1.708], $$ and $$ \lambda_a= \delta_a +1.174168821\,\delta_{a+1.80650001} +0.025921118\,\delta_{a+2.57053197} +0.118647936\,\delta_{a+2.68367709} +0.180553554\,\delta_{a+2.79017717}. $$ Writing $y=x-a$, the check becomes a one-variable positivity problem for $V(y)$ on $$ 0.708\le y\le2.806304. $$ The derivative numerator is a quartic, so the Lean files check the two endpoints, the four critical brackets, and the exact swept-interval arithmetic: Lean/Mathlib certificate package. Combined with the earlier forcing step giving $(-1.708,0)\subseteq\{U_\mu>0\}$, this gives the finite-atom update from $1.8$ to $1.806304$.

Thanks! I actually have always thought that method has potential (no pun intended), even though it doesn't directly prove the conjectured minimizer. Also, thanks for doing this rigorously, as the $1.8$ bound hasn't been properly written up before.

Thanks, natso26. Let me add a concise update. Along this finite-atom dual-forcing route, I have now pushed the checked finite certificate to\[ M=1.814600. \]First, the previous \(M=1.806304\) five-atom update can be pushed slightly further to\[ M=1.807100. \]For this improved fixed five-atom tail block, with\[ a\in[-1.807100,-1.708], \]I use\[ \lambda_a = \delta_a +1.18287976\,\delta_{a+1.80710376} +0.03349753\,\delta_{a+2.57979789} +0.11739956\,\delta_{a+2.69319012} +0.17267833\,\delta_{a+2.79229832}. \]The further update to\[ M=1.814600 \]uses a new piecewise five-atom certificate. The tail interval \([1.708,1.814600]\) is split into \(560\) blocks. On block \(i\), the same four shifts are used,\[ d_1=1.8146001,\quad d_2=2.55506,\quad d_3=2.675215475,\quad d_4=2.781815575, \]but the four nontrivial weights vary with the block:\[ \lambda_a^{(i)} = \delta_a +w^{(i)}_1\delta_{a+d_1} +w^{(i)}_2\delta_{a+d_2} +w^{(i)}_3\delta_{a+d_3} +w^{(i)}_4\delta_{a+d_4}. \]The generated required-domain check has worst margin about\[ 9.53\times 10^{-6}, \]with no bad required-domain blocks. I also tried six-atom and larger variants. In these experiments, simply adding more atoms did not help much: the extra atoms mostly became inactive, with zero or near-zero weights. The useful gain came from making the five-atom certificate piecewise in the sweep parameter. The Lean/Python files are collected here: Lean/Mathlib certificate package.

AI-assisted note: this was explored with Codex 5.5 xhigh. Please treat it as a computational/structural sanity check, not as a proof or certified update. I tried a piecewise-shift variant of your finite-atom route, since your piecewise weights seem to be doing the real work. The first naive version allowed the atom shifts d_j to vary independently from block to block, only requiring each shifted atom to remain inside its global swept lane. This gives very tempting but false numerical “certificates” around M=1.82 and higher. The failure mode is overlap: many blocks choose essentially the same favorable point near the right endpoint, so the sweep no longer produces new length. So I think the admissible piecewise-shift version needs a tiling/permutation constraint. If block i is a in [-A_i,-C_i], then the j-th shifted atom sweeps the interval [d_{j,i}-A_i, d_{j,i}-C_i]. For the sweep argument to count length, these intervals should tile the j-th lane, or at least be disjoint in a controlled way. With equal block width h, this suggests a form like d_{j,i}=A_i+s_j+pi_j(i)h, where pi_j is a permutation of the K block slots. The fixed-shift certificate is the special case pi_j(i)=i. This turns the next search into a coupled assignment problem: for each block i and each lane slot k, solve the small max-margin LP with d_{j,i}=A_i+s_j+kh, then ask whether there is a matching/permutation keeping all block margins positive. That might be a useful way to search beyond the current fixed-shift geometry without accidentally double-counting swept length. As a sanity check on tightness: using the posted fixed-shift geometry, pushing M from 1.814600 to 1.814650 already gives a negative margin about -7.9e-5 near block 192 in my checker. Increasing the number of blocks did not remove that obstruction. So any real improvement probably needs a new lane geometry or the permutation/assignment version above, not just more subdivision.

I extended the finite-atom tail certificate from 1.8146 to 1.814605. Same piecewise structure as Hua Xu's package (4 positive lanes / 5 atoms, K=560 blocks), per-block weights re-solved by LP. Both computational legs are checked end-to-end in directed interval arithmetic (IEEE-1788, outward rounding, fail-closed): all 560 sweep blocks certify positive (worst certified interval bound 7.21e-9), and the forcing certificate is regenerated over the full a ∈ [−1.708, −√2] at cap 1.836 (6504 boxes, worst 1.57e-6), re-verified with a second interval backend. The forcing leg doesn't depend on M, so — taking the thread's composition step as stated — it composes with any sweep value up to the cap; the cap is slack here (1.8146 ≪ 1.836), the sweep leg is what binds. The duality step and the (−√2,0] window come from the thread as-is (Tao's support reduction; jspier's two-atom argument, written out by natso26). The interesting part is why it stops there. At M = 1.814606 the certificate fails at −1.0e-6 and polishing can't move it. Jointly re-optimizing the four lane positions, adding a 6th atom, refining the block partition — every direction I tried closes. The binding structure is an equalization between two block families (the M-endpoint block and the plateau in the sweep blocks near a ≈ −1.78); every extra degree of freedom I added got priced to zero where it'd need to act. The searches are local, so that's evidence of a family ceiling, not exhaustion. catsflowers5544: I checked the tiling question directly (full block-by-slot margin matrices at K = 16/24/60, identity probe at K = 560): it kills the false certificates near 1.82, but no single-block reassignment I tested opens headroom — in those matrices the binding M-endpoint block already holds its best slot. I also re-checked the published 1.8146 package with the same tooling: 560/560 sweep blocks and 926/926 forcing regions certify positive, zero disagreements — both legs check out under this tooling. Certificates and Python verifiers: https://github.com/MendozaLab/erdos-experiments/tree/main/Erdos1038/lower-bound-1814605. Both legs re-check in pure Python from the bundle alone — the sweep leg via an included mpmath interval verifier that reproduces the worst block's certified bound to ~8 digits. The bounds were originally produced by a Rust interval kernel whose source isn't included; nothing rests on trusting it. Done with Claude (Anthropic) orchestrating the LP search and the Rust kernel; quoted numbers come from the independent checkers. What I couldn't settle: can a different dual family (non-atomic, or atoms off the lane structure) get past the two-family equalization, or is ~1.8146 near what dual-forcing gives?

Thanks! One of these days (but not today) we may want to have a proper writeup + packaging of the further progress on the $\inf$ lower bound to the now claimed $1.814605$. I want to draw attention to the "purple prose" here (overly flowery writing that distracts from the content due to its style), which sometimes happens in AI writing. For example, what is a "leg"? (A casework?) What does it mean for interval arithmetic to be "fail-closed"? (It means for a system to shut down on failure - but this is meaningless here.) What is an interval "backend"? What does "the sweep leg is what binds" mean? (There's a computational step referred to as "sweep", but it's not immediately clear what that one is, and this step seems to constrain the problem.) This here "...every extra degree of freedom I added got priced to zero where it'd need to act. The searches are local, so that's evidence of a family ceiling, not exhaustion" is a lot of words that mean very little. (Adding extra degree of freedom doesn't improve the numerical optimum.) In the readme, as well. A lot of space is wasted on e.g. clarifying that this is not a writeup and not a formal proof. (Why is this relevant?) It states that Hua Xu's is not re-verified, but also states that there is re-verification of Hua Xu. (This contradicts itself.) What does it mean for a forcing certificate to "compose with any sweep value"? etc.! So I'm not against AI writing per se, but I think any piece of writing must accomplish its goal of communication. And I think this is not well accomplished here.

Fair point — thanks. To restate plainly: we re-verify our own lower bound (1.814605) to ~8 digits via the included mpmath interval verifier. We do not re-verify Hua Xu's result; we only cite it. "Sweep" refers to [the specific block-by-block computation X]; there are two such computations ("legs"), each independently confirmed positive. I'll revise the readme to remove the contradictory verification language and the undefined terms.

GPT-5.4 Thinking has written up and completed some material in this thread for the $\inf$ problem in these progress notes. Summary of material: - Tao's notes prove some results for a minimizer $\mu$ with a convenient assumption of minimal variance. It turns out this assumption can be removed. So for a minimizer $\mu$, it must be a point mass in each interval of $E_\mu := \{ U_\mu > 0 \}$. Moreover, up to reflection and translation, $\mu(\{-1\}) \ge 1/2$ and $\text{supp }\mu \subseteq \{-1\} \cup [0, 1]$. - Under some regularity assumptions on $\mu$, first variation is worked out. Let $$ \lambda_\partial := \sum_{a \in \text{bd }E_\mu} \frac{1}{|U'_\mu(a)|} \delta_a, \quad Z_0 := \{ U_\mu = 0 \} \setminus \text{bd }E_\mu. $$ Then there is $c \ge 0$ and measure $\xi$ supported on $Z_0$, not both zero, such that $$ \lambda := c \lambda_\partial + \xi $$ satisfies: 1) $U_\lambda \ge 0$ in $[-1, 1] \setminus \{ U_\mu = 0 \}$ and Lebesgue-a.e.; 2) $U_\lambda = 0$ $\mu$-a.e. - We do calculations related to the ansatz we're doing in the thread, recovering the description of the conjectured minimizer. We also record an alternative transport viewpoint using pushforward measure that potentially does similar things.
I am curious: what is the latest status for this conjecture? That is, what are the best known bounds, what is expected to be true, and what is still missing?

The current commentary accurately records what we actually know for sure: the sup is known to be exactly $2\sqrt{2}$, and the inf is between 1.519 and 1.835; these notes basically contain these results. The upper bound is likely to be sharp, but we do not yet have a proof. One needs to construct a dual measure for each candidate set $E$ of measure at most $1.835$, namely a probability measure $\nu$ supported outside $E$ for which $U_\nu$ is non-negative on $[-1,1]$. We can do this when $E$ is an interval, and it seems from numerics and theory that we can also do it when $E$ is the union of a large interval and a small interval (which was the most likely candidate for a counterexample to the conjecture to be located), but we don't have a clear way forward to construct this dual measure for general $E$. (But in the case where $E$ is the union of finitely many intervals, it seems the candidate measure should have a Cauchy-Stieltjes transform that looks something like a square root of a rational function with singularities at the endpoints of the intervals.)
As a warmup, GPT-5.4 Thinking did some check on this note. Good news is there is no substantive error found. There are some minor things to fix, but we don't have to fix them now.

Professor Tao mentioned that the candidate dual measure $\lambda^{(\varepsilon)}$ should have a Cauchy–Stieltjes transform resembling a square root of a rational function with singularities at the interval endpoints. Has anyone attempted to make this ansatz explicit for the two-interval case (one large interval and one small interval near $1 - \varepsilon$)? The numerical evidence from the LP and AlphaEvolve work seems to consistently produce measures with that shape; a few atoms plus a continuous part with square-root behavior. It seems like writing down a closed-form family parametrised by $\varepsilon$ and verifying $U_{\lambda^{(\varepsilon)}} \geq 0$ analytically might be more tractable than pushing the numerics further. Is the obstacle here that the atom locations don't have clean closed forms, or is there a deeper issue?

I'm working on writing up the first variation result outlined in this thread. Interestingly, GPT-5.4 Thinking has already found a gap. I used an "arbitrary" test measure $
u$ to conclude $U_\lambda \ge 0$ from $\int U_\lambda \,d
u \ge 0$. But there was a Hahn-Banach modification which constrains $
u$ to not really be arbitrary! Good news is this appears to be fixable with more arguments and hypotheses.

Against all (or at least some) odds, I have constructed a dual measure $\lambda^{(\varepsilon)}$ for $\varepsilon = 0.002$. We have $\min U_{\lambda^{(\varepsilon)}} \approx 1.5 \cdot 10^{-4}$ at $t \approx 0.989$. That the minimum is positive has been validated on at least an order of $10^5$ points (actually more and with a few other methods). In general, it is difficult to validate such things because the optimization generally overfits to the sampling, and "denser" sampling can reveal that the solution is actually invalid. (This is a general property optimization methods, which many people misinterpret in the case of AI as the AI being "tricky" or something like that.) However, in this case we seem to have enough margin (on the order of $10^{-4}$), and enough things check out that I think we can be reasonably confident this is valid. This seems notable because AlphaEvolve was not able to achieve this task after running for over 24 hours. The algorithm is the "standard" cutting plane method for solving LP. However, it underwent several rounds of practical improvements, some coming from human suggestions. This highlights an interesting phenomenon that humans still possess some kind of "intangible" cognitive abilities that allow one to succeed in tasks that current LLMs may get stuck, even though the human does not clearly possess any extra knowledge in particular. On the other hand, without LLMs, I can say with confidence that this task is way out of reach for me, after seeing what is required in the final code. In any case, the final code is a huge mess, and will need some improvements before I try to tackle lower $\varepsilon$ such as $0.001$. The runtime for $\varepsilon = 0.002$ is 20-25 minutes and could go up with lower $\varepsilon$. Description of $\lambda^{(\varepsilon)}$; Plot of $\lambda^{(\varepsilon)}$; Plot of $U_{\lambda^{(\varepsilon)}}$; Plot of $U_{\lambda^{(\varepsilon)}}$ (zoom); ChatGPT; Colab.

Interesting, that is a bug in the verifier, I will have to work to debug it. (The verifier can be either vibecoded through the interface or manually edited.) I did undersample in the region $[1-\varepsilon,1]$ but I was sure that I at least verified it at $x=1$; I'll have to run the verifier offline to see what happened. Minimum: $U_\min \approx 1.2 \cdot 10^{-4}$ at $t \approx 0.904$. Description of $\lambda$; Plot of $\lambda$; Plot of $U_{\lambda}$; Plot of $U_{\lambda}$ (zoom). Thanks for this! I'm suspecting that the optimal $\lambda = \lambda^{(\varepsilon)}$ takes an explicit form, relating to a function of the form $$ f(z) = \frac{z+A_\varepsilon}{(z-(x_L+\varepsilon)) (z-x_R) (z-1)} \sqrt{ (z-a) (z-1+\varepsilon)} $$ for some parameter $A_\varepsilon$ (I'm guessing it is a little bit larger than 1.2, but not sure on this - $-A_\varepsilon$ should be where $U_{\lambda^{(\varepsilon)}}$ attains a local minimum) and a suitable choice of square root. The measure $\lambda$ should be like the imaginary part of this function, divided by $\pi$, so should take the form $$ \lambda = \mathrm{Res}(f;x_L+\varepsilon) \delta_{x_L+\varepsilon} + \mathrm{Res}(f; x_R) \delta_{x_R} + g(x) 1_{[a,1-\varepsilon]}(x)\ dx$$ (possibly up to sign error) with $g(x) = \frac{x+A_\varepsilon}{\pi (x-(x_L+\varepsilon)) (x-x_R) (x-1)} \sqrt{(x-a) (x-1+\varepsilon)}$, and its Hilbert transform $H\lambda$ should look like the real part, thus (up to some possible sign error and/or factor of $\pi$) $$ H\lambda = \frac{x+A_\varepsilon}{(x-(x_L+\varepsilon))(x-x_R) (x-1)} \sqrt{|x-a| |x-1-\varepsilon|} 1_{x \notin [a, 1-\varepsilon]}.$$ As usual, one should try to set $A_\varepsilon$ so that $U_\lambda$ vanishes on $[a,1-\varepsilon]$. I think things are set up so that the derivative $-H\lambda$ of $U_\lambda$ is positive just to the right of $1-\varepsilon$ and negative just to the left of $a$, so that we get a good shot at positivity of $U_\lambda$. Note in this ansatz that the densty $g$ of $\lambda$ does not become singular at the right-endpoint $1-\varepsilon$ of the interval $[a,1-\varepsilon]$, in contrast to the $\varepsilon=1$ case where there is a square root blowup at $1$. Instead we have a square root vanishing at $1-\varepsilon$ but also a simple pole at $1$ which in the limit $\varepsilon \to 0$ should recover the behavior of $\lambda^{(0)}$. At this point I'm not confident in my ability to avoid sign errors, but given that ChatGPT was able to previously work out a viable $\lambda$ in the $\varepsilon=0$ case, it might be able to confirm this ansatz for positive $\varepsilon$ as well. Previously my ansatz was losing a factor of $\sqrt{\varepsilon}$ when evaluating $U_\lambda$ near $1$, but here I think one is instead gaining a factor of this form, so one is potentially in better shape (though I don't understand what $U_\lambda$ is doing near the opposite endpoint -1, which is the other place where positivity could be in doubt.)

Some progress on this. In short: I'm not sure if such a measure $\lambda^{(\varepsilon)}$ exists for all $\varepsilon > 0$ whose support avoids $(x_L+\varepsilon,x_R) \cup (1-\varepsilon,1)$ with the property that $U_\lambda \ge 0$ on $[-1,1]$. First, in order to perform these computations we need the conjectured minimizer to higher precision. We worked this out to be: $$ \begin{align*} & a \in [0.804461, 0.804463], A \in [0.8245215, 0.8245220], \\ & x_L \in [-1.8081076, -1.8081072], x_R \in [0.0263228, 0.0263233]. \end{align*} $$ This should be useful for other purposes as well (such as running AlphaEvolve for smaller $\varepsilon$). In practice we take the midpoint i.e. $x_L = -1.8081074$, $x_R = 0.02632305$. $\textbf{First try}$. We put $\text{supp }\lambda$ on the endpoints $\{x_L+\varepsilon,x_R,1-\varepsilon,1\}$ exactly ($4$ atoms). Then it reduces to a linear programming (LP) problem, and we use the cutting-plane method. This turns out to work for large $\varepsilon$ but not smaller $\varepsilon$. Specifically, it works for $\varepsilon \in \{0.1,0.05\}$ but doesn't work for $\varepsilon \in \{0.02,0.01,0.005,\dots\}$. $\textbf{Second try}$. We add another atom in the interval $(x_R, 1-\varepsilon)$ to $\text{supp }\lambda$. For each position of this atom, we run an LP algorithm. A coarse sweep works for $\varepsilon = 0.02$ but not $\varepsilon = 0.01$. Then, we do local refinement to target the best region, which works for $\varepsilon = 0.01$ but not for $\varepsilon = 0.005$. $\textbf{Third try}$. We add $2$ atoms. But now optimization becomes tricky. We employ the Nelder-Mead method to optimize, but it still doesn't work for $\varepsilon = 0.005$. $\textbf{Fourth try}$. We use support augmentation: we start with $4$ original atoms, find the "weakest" position where $U_\lambda$ is minimized, and put an atom there. Then we iterate until $U_\lambda \ge 0$ on $[-1,1]$. For $\varepsilon = 0.005$, a solution was found with $10$ atoms. For $\varepsilon = 0.002$, the algorithm unfortunately stagnates at $26$ atoms and was not able to find a solution. $\textbf{Conclusion}$. I believe I have numerical evidence that such a measure $\lambda^{(\varepsilon)}$ exists for large $\varepsilon$, specifically $\varepsilon \ge 0.005$. But I am not sure whether it exists for all $\varepsilon > 0$; perhaps the strategy so far is not smart enough in some way. In particular, AlphaEvolve might be smarter! Details can be found in this ChatGPT chat.

Very nice natso26! I wonder whether it suffices to obtain $U_\lambda > 0$ only on $\{-1\} \cup [0,1]$, since Tao showed that one may assume the support of a minimizing measure $\mu$ is contained in this set. If so, there may be room to take $\varepsilon$ smaller. In fact, using this observation, I was able to obtain a small improvement to the $\inf$ lower bound using two atoms, showing that it is at least $1.614$ and that we may assume $(-1.53,0] \subseteq \{ U_\mu > 0 \}$. I am quite confident that this can be further improved by considering three or four atoms. First, we performed a search for useful measures $
u = \delta_a + B\,\delta_b$, with $a \le 0 \le b$ and $B > 0$, such that $U_
u(x) > 0$ for all $x \in [-1,1]$. I asked ChatGPT to generate code for a simple brute-force search and then modified it slightly for efficiency, it is available here. This produced the admissible region for $(a,b)$ shown here. The minimum possible value of $a$ is $-1.518$ at $b = 0.395$, in agreement with J_Koizumi_144’s approach to the $\inf$ lower bound. Next, we searched for measures $
u$ of the same form, but requiring only that $U_
u(x) > 0$ for $x \in \{-1\} \cup [0,1]$. This yields a larger admissible region, shown here. The minimum possible value of $a$ is now $-1.614$, attained at $b = 0.60$. For each $a \in [-1.614,0]$, there is a corresponding interval $(b_{\min}(a), b_{\max}(a))$ of admissible values of $b$. Now assume that the supremum of $[-1.614,-\sqrt{2}] \setminus \{ U_\mu > 0 \}$ exists and equals $a_0$. Then, by the duality principle, we have $(a_0,0) \sqcup (b_{\min}(a_0), b_{\max}(a_0)) \subseteq \{ U_\mu > 0 \}$, and hence $-a_0 + b_{\max}(a_0) - b_{\min}(a_0) \leq \lvert \{ U_\mu > 0 \} vert$. Plotting $-a + b_{\max}(a) - b_{\min}(a)$, we find that it is at least $1.835$ for $a \in [-1.53,-\sqrt{2}]$, and that it decreases to $1.614$ as $a$ decreases further. This establishes the claimed bounds.

Ok, I think the logic is correct. Anyone else is welcome to check. Logic: Let $\mu$ be a minimizer. By Tao's results, $\mu$ is supported on $\{-1\} \cup [0,1]$. Assume for contradiction that $|\{U_\mu > 0\}| < 1.614$. Then the largest interval $(a_0, 0] \subset \{U_\mu > 0\}$ must have $a_0 < 1.614$. I think there is some confusion about this problem. Problem 1 of [EHP58] is asking about the infimum/supremum of $|\{x\in\mathbb{R}: |f(x)|<1\}|$ over all monic polynomials $f$ with all its (real) roots contained in $[-1,1]$. There's no dependence on the degree of a polynomial, and I was kind of confused by the current statement on the website when I was trying to formalize this in Lean. Problem 2 of the same source is asking about the infimum of $|\{x\in\mathbb{C}: |f(x)|<1\}|$ over all monic polynomials $f$ of degree $n$ with all its roots contained in the closed unit disk of the complex plane (note that there's a dependence on the degree of a polynomial here). This is an entirely different problem as it is asking about the two dimensional measure, and Pommerenke's result is about the two dimensional measure. I have explored this notion of "dual measure" $\lambda$ associated with a candidate minimizing measure $\mu$, with ChatGPT. First, recall that we claim that a measure $\lambda := \lambda(\mu)$ can be constructed possessing the properties: 1) $U_\lambda \geq 0$ on $[-1,1]$. 2) $U_\lambda = 0$ $\mu$-almost everywhere. 3) $\text{supp }\lambda \subset \{U = 0\}$. More precisely, $$ \lambda = \sum_{a \in \text{bd}(\{U > 0\})} \frac{1}{|U_\mu'(a)|} \delta_a + \xi, \quad \text{supp }\xi \subset \{U = 0\} \setminus \text{bd}(\{U > 0\}). $$ $\textbf{Two Diracs}$. The case of $\mu_p = (1-p)\delta_{-1} + p\delta_1$. In this case, Tao has computed $p \approx 0.1459$ is minimizing within this family. However, numerically, the constructed $\lambda$ according to the formula above does not satisfy the three properties. More precisely, it is indeed true that $U_\lambda(-1) \approx 0$, $U_\lambda(1) \approx 0$ as required. But it is not true that $U_\lambda \geq 0$ on $[-1,1]$; it dips to $\approx -0.23$ somewhere near $1$. Quick numerical check confirms this is not rounding error but is truly negative, but I did not attempt to confirm any digit. See images Plot for $U_\mu$, Plot for $U_\lambda$. This means that, by first variation, this $\mu_p$ for $p \approx 0.1459$ is not a local minimizer. $\textbf{Corollary}$. Any linear combination of two Diracs is not a local minimizer. $\textbf{Proof}$. If the two Diracs are not at $-1,1$, we can pull them apart. Let's look at the interval of $\{U > 0\}$ containing $-1$. Suppose this interval is fixed and we move $1$ rightward. This decreases the potential $U_\mu$ at the endpoints; therefore the endpoints must move closer to $-1$, decreasing its length. By symmetry, the interval containing $1$ also decreases its length. Hence this is not a local minimizer. Now suppose the two Diracs are at $-1,1$. By Tao's calculations, the only possibility for a local minimizer is at $p \approx 0.1459$. But we have argued above this is not a local minimizer, either. So two Diracs are not local minimizers. $\blacksquare$ $\textbf{Conjectured global minimizer}$. This is $$ \mu = A\delta_{-1} + f(x) 1_{[a,1]}(x) \,dx, \quad f(x) = \frac{1}{\pi}\cdot \frac{x + (1 - A\sqrt{2(1+a)})}{(x+1) \sqrt{(1-x)(x-a)}}, $$ $a \approx 0.805$, $A \approx 0.825$. The dual measure $\lambda$ has an explicit form $$ \lambda = \zeta_L\delta_{x_L} + \zeta_R\delta_{x_R} + g(x) 1_{[a,1]} \, dx. $$ Here $x_L \approx -1.808$, $x_R \approx 0.026$ are the two boundary points of $\{U_\mu > 0\}$. We have $\zeta_L = 1/|U_\mu'(x_L)| \approx 0.922$, $\zeta_R = 1/|U_\mu'(x_R)| \approx 1.653$. And $$ g(x) = \frac{1}{\pi \sqrt{(1-x)(x-a)}} \left( c_0 + \frac{\zeta_L R(x_L)}{x-x_L} + + \frac{\zeta_R R(x_R)}{x-x_R} \right), $$ where $R(z) = \sqrt{(z-a)(z-1)}$ is taken on the branch with $R(z) \sim z$ as $z \to \infty$; for real $x<a$ this is $R(x) = -\sqrt{(x-a)(x-1)}$. Finally, $c_0 \approx 2.805$ is chosen so that $U_\lambda(x) = 0$ for $x \in [a,1]$. Note that no attempts were made to confirm the numerical digits. This $\lambda$ satisfies all three properties, as numerically verified by code. See also the Plot for $U_\mu$ and $U_\lambda$. Regarding Tao's comment about Schwarz-Christoffel mapping, ChatGPT explains that "...the square-root endpoints $a,1$ behave like SC “prevertices” with exponent $-1/2$..."; the full explanation is in the chat but I do not claim accuracy.

Thanks for doing the (ChatGPT-assisted) analysis! By the way, I think the $U_\lambda$ plot in the two Diracs case is just a repeat of the $U_\mu$ plot with different axes (a ChatGPT hallucination perhaps?). The dual function $\lambda = \zeta_L \delta_{x_L} + \zeta_R \delta_{X_R} + g(x) 1_{[a,1]}\ dx$ could be the key to actually showing that the conjectural extremizer $\mu$ is the actual infimum. Suppose we have some competing extremizer $\tilde \mu = \mu + \eta$ to $\mu$, which we can take to have a similar structure of a large mass at $-1$ and some remaining mass somewhere in $(0,1]$, so $U_{\tilde \mu} = U_\mu + U_\eta$ (with $\eta$ a signed measure of total variation at most $2$) similarly is positive on a large interval containing $(-1,0)$ but also additionally is positive on $\{ x \in [a,1]: U_\eta > 0 \}$. Morally speaking, the effect of $U_\eta$ on the large interval is to increase its length by $$ \approx \int U_\eta\ d(\zeta_L \delta_{x_L} + \zeta_R \delta_{x_R})$$ although this is just the linearized (first variation) approximation and there is a correction term I don't understand yet (though the convexity properties of $U_\mu$ suggest that the correction term may mostly have a favorable sign). Ignoring this correction for now, we can write this increase as $$ \int U_\eta\ d\lambda - \int_a^1 U_\eta(x) g(x)\ dx = \int U_\lambda(x)\ d\eta - \int_a^1 U_\eta(x) g(x)\ dx.$$ So, morally speaking, to establish the global infimum, one needs an inequality of the form $$ \int_a^1 U_\eta(x) g(x)\ dx \leq |\{ x \in [a,1]: U_\eta(x) > 0\}| + \int U_\lambda(x)\ d\eta$$ (One could also add in the contribution in the region between $[a,1]$ and the large interval where $U_\mu + U_\eta$ becomes positive, but this looks tricky to exploit.) The term $U_\lambda(x)\ d\eta$ is non-negative (and would vanish if the modification $\eta$ is restricted to $\{-1\} \cup [a,1]$), so the main issue is to see to what extent $\int_a^1 U_\eta(x) g(x)\ dx$ is controlled by $|\{ x \in [a,1]: U_\eta(x) > 0\}|$. If $U_\eta$ and $g$ were both bounded (by sufficiently small constants) then there would be a clear route to getting this control. Unfortunately $g$ has a mild singularity at the endpoints of $[a,1]$, and $U_\eta$ also can have logarithmic singularities. However, $U_\eta$ is of bounded mean oscillation (BMO), and thus obeys distributional inequalities such as the John-Nirenberg inequality that can be used in some applications as a substitute for boundedness. It may be that one has to do some brute force numerics to cover the space of all possible $\eta$ by some finite number of balls in weak topologies and verify the required inequality to sufficient accuracy on each ball, although first one needs to control the error in the linearized analysis. I am on vacation right now, but in a few days I might set up some further AlphaEvolve experiments to see if there is a chance to prove inequalities such as $\int_a^1 U_\eta(x) g(x)\ dx \leq |\{ x \in [a,1]: U_\eta(x) > 0\}|$ which the above analysis suggests would go a long way to establishing the global infimum property.

I don't think one can proceed purely through variations here, because the quantity $|\{ U_{\tilde \mu} > 0\}|$ does not vary in a differentiable (or even continuous) fashion with respect to $\tilde \mu$. In particular, if one were to consider a path of measures $\mu + t\eta$ with $0 \leq t \leq 1$, the quantity $|\{ U_{\mu+t\eta} > 0\}|$ would immediately experience a jump discontinuity by $\{ x \in [a,1]: U_\eta(x) > 0\}$ as soon as $t$ became positive, though it may possibly decline afterwards as $t$ continues onwards to $1$. So one has to somehow compare the $t=0$ and $t=1$ cases directly rather than try to show that some derivative has a favorable sign along the path. On the other hand, I think we may at least be able to solve the model problem in which we assume in advance that $\{ U_{\tilde \mu} > 0\}$ is an interval. Specifically, the existence of the dual measure $\lambda$ to the putative minimizer $\mu$ already shows that any competitor set $\{ U_{\tilde \mu} > 0\}$ cannot be a proper subinterval of the interval $\{ U_\mu > 0 \}$, for if this were the case then $\int U_{\tilde \mu}\ d\lambda$ woud be negative, which makes $\int U_\lambda\ d\tilde \mu$ negative, which is absurd since $U_\lambda$ is non-negative on $[0,1]$. If one can find other dual weights $\lambda$ corresponding to various shifts $(-1.808+\delta,0.026+\delta)$ of the interval $(-1.808, 0.026) = \{ U_\mu > 0 \}$ (i.e. measures supported outside of these intervals with $U_\lambda \geq 0$ on $[-1,1]$), then one could rule out $\{ U_{\tilde \mu}>0\}$ being equal to any interval that is strictly shorter than $(-1.808, 0.026).

One consequence of this: Away from $\text{supp }\lambda$, $U_\lambda$ is strictly convex. So in each open interval of $[-1,1] \setminus \text{supp }\lambda$, $U_\lambda = 0$ at at most one point. Since $U_\lambda = 0$ $\mu$-a.e., $\text{supp }\mu$ has at most one point in each open interval of $[-1,1] \setminus \text{supp }\lambda$. So $\mu$ must be a combination of Diracs and something supported on $\{U_\lambda = 0\}$. A Dirac gives $+\infty$ potential near it, so it's in an interval of $\{U_\mu > 0\}$. Conversely you've shown each interval of $\{U_\mu > 0\}$ has exactly one Dirac. For points that are isolated in $\{U_\mu = 0\}$, $\mu$ can't have support on them because if so these must be Diracs, but Diracs must be in intervals of $\{U_\mu > 0\}$. Therefore: $\mu$ must have the following structure. It's a combination of Diracs, which map one-to-one with open intervals of $\{U_\mu > 0\}$ containing each one; plus something supported in the region $\{U_\mu = 0\}$ excluding its isolated points. (In short: Diracs plus "flat regions" which make $U_\mu = 0$.) If this is correct, it gives some structure to work with. However: as with isoperimetric-type problems, once we've reached this stage the configuration is probably resistant to naive variations already. (In proof of Double Bubble conjecture, they quickly established constraints like a minimizer must be a surface of revolution; but this still retains many nonstandard configurations that are difficult to rule out.) So this is roughly the limits of what this kind of argument should be able to do with this amount of effort. But there could be other clever ways to finish it off from here, with or without using variations.

Ah, I realize now why your numbers are different from mine; there is an additional difficulty that shows up in the general case of a measure $\sqrt{2}$ set in $[-M,+\infty)$ compared to that of the model case $[-M,-M+2\sqrt{2}]$. It has to do with the question of lower bounding $U_{1_E}(x)$ when $E$ is some measure $L$ set contained in $[-M+a, +\infty]$ (in our application we take $L = 2\sqrt{2}-a$). The model case of $E$ is $[-M+a,-M+a+L]$, and we would like to have the lower bound $U_{1_E}(x) \geq U_{1_{[-M+a,-M+a+L]}}(x)$ as this would imply that the interval $[-M+a,-M+a+L]$ is the worst case. But the Hardy-Littlewood rearrangement inequality only guarantees this as long as $x$ is to the left of the midpoint $-M+a+\frac{L}{2}$. To the right of this interval, we only get the weaker lower bound $U_{1_E}(x) \geq U_{1_{[-M+a,-M+a+L]}}(-M+a+\frac{L}{2})$. So to handle the general case with $\nu = \delta_{-M} + A 1_E + C \delta_m$, it is not enough to obtain the lower bound $$ U_{\delta_{-M}}(x) + A U_{1_{[-M+a,-M+2\sqrt{2}]}}(x) + C U_{\delta_{-M+2\sqrt{2}}}(x) \geq 0$$ for $x \in [-1,1]$; one has to obtain the slightly stronger bound $$ U_{\delta_{-M}}(x) + A U_{1_{[-M+a,-M+2\sqrt{2}]}}(\min(x,-M+\frac{a}{2}+\sqrt{2})) + C U_{\delta_{-M+2\sqrt{2}}}(x) \geq 0.$$ It's quite possible that one can do better with more complex $\nu$, but the rearrangement analysis becomes trickier. Let the masses be $x_1,\dots,x_n$ with weights $w_1,\dots,w_n$. We consider $$ U(a, X) = \sum_j w_j \log |a - x_j|. $$ $X$ is the parameters $(x, w)$. Suppose we're at an optimizer which is a "smooth interior point" (not well defined at this point). Consider $a$ which is an endpoint of an interval, i.e. $U(a, X) = 0$. If I move $X$ while maintaining this constraint, we have $$ da = -[1/(\partial U/\partial a)] (\partial U/\partial X)dX. $$ Then $$ d(\text{interval_lengths}) = \sum_a \text{sgn}(\partial U/\partial a) da = \sum_a -[1/|\partial U/\partial a|] (\partial U/\partial X)dX = 0. $$ Now we can't assume much, only that there is an $i$ such that $x_i \neq 1, -1$ and that $\partial U/\partial a \neq 0$. Then we can move $x_i$ and $w_i$ freely in a local region (only two coords). Unfortunately that equation doesn't seem to give a contradiction as such. However I was able to salvage the following argument. Note $$ \frac{\partial U}{\partial w_i}(a, X) = \log |a - x_i|. $$ Then $$ \frac{d(\text{interval_lengths})}{dw_i} = - \sum_a \frac{\log |a - x_i|}{|\partial U/\partial a|}. $$ Now suppose there is an invisible $x_{i,L}$ and $x_{i,R}$ near $x_i$ with weight $0$. If this configuration is a maximizer, we must have $$ \frac{d(\text{interval_lengths})}{dw_{i,L}} \leq 0, \quad \frac{d(\text{interval_lengths})}{dw_i} = 0, \quad \frac{d(\text{interval_lengths})}{dw_{i,R}} \leq 0. $$ But $x \mapsto -\log |x|$ is strictly convex. So you can't actually have those three things simultaneously satisfied. Conclusion: for a maximizer (this doesn't work for minimizer), you can't move the parameters that way. For the case that some $a$ has $\partial U/\partial a = 0$: this is actually even easier because an interval is coming into existence and the length is increasing like $O(\sqrt \varepsilon)$ which dominates $O(\varepsilon)$ from other $a$'s. This seems to roughly prove that a maximizer must consist of atoms at $1,-1$ plus "potential-zero regions" only. But I see that the interpretation of "potential-zero region" for discrete masses is not exactly well defined in terms of what I wrote above. So I think this doesn't cleanly "prove" it yet, due to the discrete and continuous cases not being exactly analogous? But perhaps this is fixable?

On taking logarithms and normalizing by the degree, one sees that the question is equivalent to finding the infimum or supremum of $| \{ x: U_\mu(x) < 0 \}|$, where $\mu$ ranges over probability measures in $[-1,1]$, and $U_\mu(x) = \int \log |x-y|\ d\mu(y)$ is the logarithmic potential. (A regularization and approximation argument allows one to restrict to those $\mu$ that are discrete with rational probability masses, and then they are the empirical zero distribution of some high-degree polynomial.) AlphaEvolve gave this example of a probability measure (expressed as a pairs of points together with the mass associated to that point) that showed that the infimum can be as low as 1.84: [(-0.84, 0.01607282659569635), (-0.87, 0.018668276991332278), (-0.96, 0.015722736638053406), (-1.0, 0.02786639212010361), (-0.94, 0.016135713032333365), (-0.91, 0.018142805612153572), (1.0, 0.8305116235730803), (-0.8, 0.026585242076298716), (-0.99, 0.013889666827470722), (-0.98, 0.016404716533477668)]. (Caveat: I used a crude discretization method to estimate the size of the level set, so I do not claim more than two decimal points of precision here.) Readers are invited to "beat the AI" on this! EDIT: here is an image of what the log potential of this candidate minimizer looks like. On the other hand, the following simple argument shows that the infimum must be at least 1. For any $0 < a < 1$, the polynomial $(a-x)(-a-x)$ has absolute value strictly less than $1$ on $[-1,1]$, hence $|f(a) f(-a)| < 1$ for all monic polynomials $f$ with roots in $[-1,1]$ (or equivalently $U_\mu(a) + U_\mu(-a) < 0$ for all probability measures $\mu$ on $[-1,1]$). We conclude that at least one of $a,-a$ lie in $\{ x: |f(x)| < 1 \}$ (or $\{ x: U_\mu(x) < 0 \}$), giving the lower bound of $1$. Probably one can do better than this (for instance, by somehow taking advantage of the points outside of $[-1,1]$). On the supremum side, AlphaEvolve seems convinced that $2 \sqrt{2}$ (corresponding to the uniform distribution on $\{-1,+1\}$) is optimal. Even adding a penalty to getting too close to this distribution fails to budge AE from this unless the penalty is quite large. Not a proof, but perhaps some support for the conjecture. EDIT: AE numerics suggest that there may be a possibility of a computer assisted proof, possibly along the following lines: (a) establish the result by a rigorous perturbative analysis when the measure $\mu$ is sufficiently close to being supported on $\{-1,+1\}$, e.g., by requiring that $\int x^2\ d\mu(x)$ is sufficiently close to 1. (b) In all other cases, discretize the space of probability measures somehow (in something like the vague topology or the Levy metric, for which the space to discretize is compact) and show numerically that some robust version of the statistic $| \{ x: U_\mu(x) < 0 \}|$ is bounded away from $2 \sqrt{2}$. If the gap with $2 \sqrt{2}$ is large compared to the discretization scale, one may then be able to cover all cases.

Some further details on the strategy. Let $-M$ and $m$ denote the inf and sup of $\{x: U_\mu < 0 \}$. By symmetry we may assume $M \geq m$. If $M + m \leq 2\sqrt{2}$ then we are done, so we may assume $M + m > 2 \sqrt{2}$, so $M > \sqrt{2}$. The function $U_\mu$ is increasing on $(1,\infty)$, decreasing on $(-\infty,-1)$, and positive outside of $[-2,2]$, so we may assume that $\sqrt{2} < M < 2$, that $U_\mu(-M)=0$, and $U_\mu(-M + 2 \sqrt{2}) \leq 0$. (EDIT: technically, one needs $-M+2\sqrt{2} > 1$, or $M < 1.828$, to make this deduction, but this condition is satisfied in the analysis below, so this isn't a problem.) Numerically it seems that for $M$ in the range $\sqrt{2} < M \leq 1.7624$, that there exists some positive real $\alpha$ such that the concave function $\log |x+M| + \alpha \log |x-(-M+2\sqrt{2})|$ is positive at $x = \pm 1$, hence positive on all of $[-1,1]$. Integrating against $\mu$ we get $U_\mu(-M) + \alpha U_\mu(-M + 2 \sqrt{2}) > 0$, a contradiction. This leaves the range $1.7624 < M \leq 2$. Here one has to work harder and try to force some positivity of $U_\mu$ inside of $[-1,1]$. However one is now quite far away from the conjectural extremizer (for which $M = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$) and there should be room to play all sorts of additional tricks.

If we look at $-1 + \delta$ and $1 + \delta$, then roots in between have product of distances to these values $\leq 1$. Outside of $[-1 + \delta, 1 + \delta]$, the product is maximized at root $= -1$. So if we want $|f(-1 + \delta)f(1 + \delta)| \leq 1$ the strictest constraint is $\delta (\delta + 2) = 1$. Or $\delta = \sqrt 2 - 1$. So one of $-1 + \delta$ and $1 + \delta$ must be chosen (this utilizes values outside of $[-1, 1]$ as you suggested). Similarly we do the same thing near $1$ instead of $-1$. And in the middle $[-1 + \delta, 1 - \delta]$ we use the original $-a,a$ strategy. This gives combined length at least $\delta + \delta + (1 - \delta) = \sqrt 2$. So the inf is $\geq \sqrt 2$. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

It seems that the statement you pointed out, namely that the infimum is at least $\sqrt{2}$, is already proved as Theorem 1 in [EHP58]. More precisely, it is shown that if the mean of the roots of $f(x)$ is nonnegative, then the set $\{x:|f(x)|<1\}$ contains an interval of length at least $\sqrt{2}$ that contains $(0,1)$. However, I think that by extending your (and TerenceTao’s) argument to cubic polynomials as follows, one can even prove that the infimum is at least $2^{4/3}-1 = 1.519842\cdots$. (EDIT: We first assume by symmetry that the mean of the roots of $f(x)$ is nonnegative.) Let $\alpha=2^{4/3}=2.519\cdots$ and $\beta=2^{-2/3}=0.629\cdots$. Consider the function $g(x)=(x-\alpha)(x-\beta)^2$. It satisfies $-1<g(x)<0$ for almost every $x\in[0,\alpha]$. Hence, for each $x\in[-1,1]$, we have $|g(x+\delta)|<1$ for almost every $\delta\in[1,\alpha-1]$. Since\[ g(x+\delta)=(\alpha-\delta-x)(\beta-\delta-x),\quad (\text{EDIT: this should be }(\alpha-\delta-x)(\beta-\delta-x)^2) \]it follows that for almost every $\delta\in[1,\alpha-1]$ we have $|f(\alpha-\delta)f(\beta-\delta)|<1$ (EDIT: this should be $|f(\alpha-\delta)f(\beta-\delta)^2|<1$). This means that more than half of the set $[1,\alpha-1]\cup[\beta-\alpha+1,\beta-1]$ is contained in $\{x: |f(x)|<1\}$. Combining this with the above-mentioned theorem (or TerenceTao's argument) gives the desired claim.

Nice! Two corrections: in order to use Theorem 1 of EHP58 one should first use symmetry to reduce to the case of non-negative mean; and secondly I think you need to square the factor $\beta-\delta-x$ and $f(\beta-\delta)$ in your argument. (I think my original argument's can't supply a measure of $1$ in the remaining region $[\beta-1,1]$, which in any event would require some additional hypothesis on the polynomial, as the example $x+1$ shows.) The full proof of Theorem 1 of EHP58 is lengthy, but the portion needed here, namely that the zero set contains [0,1] if the mean of the zeroes is non-negative (and contains [-1,0] instead of the mean is non-positive) is a simple application of Jensen's inequality. In the language of log potentials: if the barycenter $\overline{t} := \int t\ d\mu(t)$ is non-negative, then for any $x \in [0,1]$ we have from Jensen $$ U_\mu(x) = \int \log |x-t|\ d\mu(t) \leq \log \int |x-t|\ d\mu(t) \leq \log \left(\int |x-t|\ d\mu(t) + x \overline{t}\right)$$ $$= \log \int |x-t| + x t\ d\mu \leq \log \int 1\ d\mu = 0$$ as required. EDIT: In a similar vein, Theorem 2 of EHP58 shows that the sup is at most 3. So we currently have $2^{4/3}-1 = 1.519\dots \leq \inf \leq 1.852\dots$ and $2\sqrt{2} = 2.828\dots \leq \sup \leq 3$.

Thank you for the corrections. If we set $I=[1,\alpha-1]\cup [\beta-\alpha+1,\beta-1]$, then $I\cap(-I)=\emptyset$, and by symmetry, half of $-I$ is also contained in $\{x:|f(x)|<1\}$. The remaining region lies in $[-1,1]$ and is symmetric, so I think your argument applies there. I also feel that one might be able to improve this a bit further by combining this kind of argument with the reduction step used in the proof of [EHP58, Theorem 1].

One particular step of the proof of [EHP58, Theorem 1] that might be useful for the inf problem: once one has located an interval $J$ on which $|f(x)| \leq 1$, one can replace all the zeroes in $J$ by their arithmetic mean, since by Jensen's inequality (or the AM-GM inequality) this only serves to decrease $|f(x)|$ to the left or right of $J$, and thus cannot increase the measure of the set $\{ |f(x)| \leq 1\}$. Hence without loss of generality we may assume that every interval in the set contains only one zero (possibly with high multiplicity). This is consistent with the AE examples, where we typically have a large interval containing a single mass at $\pm 1$. The [EHP58, Theorem 1] analysis also tells us that this single mass should have measure at least $1/2$, and I think wlog one can use a translation to place it at an endpoint $\pm 1$.

Gemini Deep Research concluded that this problem was a "cold case" and had no significant followup literature, despite the extensive work done on other problems raised in [EHP58]. However, it also pointed out a correction to the above: [EHP58] only proved the $2 \sqrt{2}$ upper bound under the additional assumption that all the roots lie on the endpoints $\{-1,+1\}$ of the interval, and leave open the question of obtaining this bound in the general case where the roots are also allowed to lie in the interior $(-1,1)$ as a conjecture.
